metadata.title: Proceedings on Consideration of Lords Amendments as at 12 September 2023
metadata.author: HOC

chunks.text:
Lords Amendments: Tuesday 12 September 2023

Online Safety Bill (Decisions on Motions relating to Lords Amendments) This document sets out the fate of motions considered in relation to Lords Amendments. A glossary with key terms can be found at the end of this document.

On Consideration of Lords Amendments to the Online Safety Bill Lords Amendment 182

As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan

Agreed to

(a)

Agreed to

(a)

Agreed to

(b)

Line 1, leave out ““presented by content”” and insert ““content on””

Lords Amendment, as amended, agreed to.

Lords Amendment 349

As Amendments to the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan

Line 20, at end insert— “(qa) sections 104 to 106;” Secretary Michelle Donelan

Line 24, at end insert— “(ta) sections 150 and 151;”

2

Tuesday 12 September 2023

LORDS AMENDMENTS

Lords Amendment, as amended, agreed to.

Lords Amendment 391

As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan

Agreed to

(a)

Line 1, after ““and” insert “at least one specified condition about”

Lords Amendment 391 Mr Marcus Fysh

Not selected

To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh

To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh

Not selected

On Consideration of Lords Amendments to the Online Safety Bill Lords Amendment 182

As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan

Agreed to

(a)

Agreed to

(a)

Agreed to

Tuesday 12 September 2023

LORDS AMENDMENTS

Lords Amendment, as amended, agreed to.

Lords Amendment 391

As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan

Agreed to

(a)

Line 1, after ““and” insert “at least one specified condition about”

Lords Amendment 391 Mr Marcus Fysh

Not selected

To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh

To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh

Not selected

chunks.text: ““and” insert “at least one specified condition about” Lords Amendment 391 Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected Page 217, line 7, leave out paragraph (a) and insert— “(a) in relation to the Category 1 threshold conditions— (i) at least one specified condition about number of users and at least one specified condition about functionality must be met, or (ii) at least one specified condition about number of users is met and, following an assessment, OFCOM has determined that in all the circumstances the risk of harm to individuals in the United Kingdom is such as to justify the service being included within Category 1, (aa) in relation to the Category 2B threshold conditions, at least one specified condition about number of users and at least one specified condition about functionality must be met, and” Lords Amendment, as amended, agreed to. Lords Amendment 391 Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following consequential Amendment to the Bill:— (a) Tuesday 12 September 2023 Secretary Michelle Donelan LORDS AMENDMENTS Agreed to (a) Page 78, line 21, at end insert— “(3A) If the regulations under paragraph 1(1) of Schedule 11 specify that a service meets the Category 1 threshold conditions if any one condition about number of users or functionality is met (as mentioned in paragraph 1(4)(a) of that Schedule)— (a) (b) subsection (2) applies as if paragraph (b) were omitted, and subsections (3) and (7) apply as if the reference to the conditions in subsection (2) were to the condition in subsection (2)(a).” Lords Amendment 17 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to
chunks.display_text: ““and” insert “at least one specified condition about” Lords Amendment 391 Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected Page 217, line 7, leave out paragraph (a) and insert— “(a) in relation to the Category 1 threshold conditions— (i) at least one specified condition about number of users and at least one specified condition about functionality must be met, or (ii) at least one specified condition about number of users is met and, following an assessment, OFCOM has determined that in all the circumstances the risk of harm to individuals in the United Kingdom is such as to justify the service being included within Category 1, (aa) in relation to the Category 2B threshold conditions, at least one specified condition about number of users and at least one specified condition about functionality must be met, and” Lords Amendment, as amended, agreed to. Lords Amendment 391 Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following consequential Amendment to the Bill:— (a) Tuesday 12 September 2023 Secretary Michelle Donelan LORDS AMENDMENTS Agreed to (a) Page 78, line 21, at end insert— “(3A) If the regulations under paragraph 1(1) of Schedule 11 specify that a service meets the Category 1 threshold conditions if any one condition about number of users or functionality is met (as mentioned in paragraph 1(4)(a) of that Schedule)— (a) (b) subsection (2) applies as if paragraph (b) were omitted, and subsections (3) and (7) apply as if the reference to the conditions in subsection (2) were to the condition in subsection (2)(a).” Lords Amendment 17 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to

chunks.text: (as mentioned in paragraph 1(4)(a) of that Schedule)— (a) (b) subsection (2) applies as if paragraph (b) were omitted, and subsections (3) and (7) apply as if the reference to the conditions in subsection (2) were to the condition in subsection (2)(a).” Lords Amendment 17 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Lords Amendment accordingly disagreed to. Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following Amendments to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (a) Page 9, line 30, leave out paragraph (e) and insert— “(e) the extent to which the design of the service, in particular its functionalities, affects the level of risk of harm that might be suffered by children, identifying and assessing those functionalities that present higher levels of risk, including functionalities— (i) enabling adults to search for other users of the service (including children), or (ii) enabling adults to contact other users (including children) by means of the service;” As an Amendment to Secretary Michelle Donelan’s proposed Amendment (a) in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Marcus Fysh Not called (i) Line 9, at end insert— “but not being required to identify or assess those functionalities that do not present a significant risk, including functionalities related to multi-party computation, data 3 4 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS exchange, verification or authentication functionalities used primarily for purposes other than communication of media or messaging content.” Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (b) Page 9, line 38, after “used,” insert “including functionalities or other features of the service that affect how much children use the service (for example a feature that enables content to play automatically),” Lords Amendment 20 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to
chunks.display_text: (as mentioned in paragraph 1(4)(a) of that Schedule)— (a) (b) subsection (2) applies as if paragraph (b) were omitted, and subsections (3) and (7) apply as if the reference to the conditions in subsection (2) were to the condition in subsection (2)(a).” Lords Amendment 17 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Lords Amendment accordingly disagreed to. Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following Amendments to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (a) Page 9, line 30, leave out paragraph (e) and insert— “(e) the extent to which the design of the service, in particular its functionalities, affects the level of risk of harm that might be suffered by children, identifying and assessing those functionalities that present higher levels of risk, including functionalities— (i) enabling adults to search for other users of the service (including children), or (ii) enabling adults to contact other users (including children) by means of the service;” As an Amendment to Secretary Michelle Donelan’s proposed Amendment (a) in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Marcus Fysh Not called (i) Line 9, at end insert— “but not being required to identify or assess those functionalities that do not present a significant risk, including functionalities related to multi-party computation, data 3 4 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS exchange, verification or authentication functionalities used primarily for purposes other than communication of media or messaging content.” Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (b) Page 9, line 38, after “used,” insert “including functionalities or other features of the service that affect how much children use the service (for example a feature that enables content to play automatically),” Lords Amendment 20 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to

Lords Amendment accordingly disagreed to. Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following Amendments to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Tuesday 12 September 2023 Secretary Michelle Donelan LORDS AMENDMENTS Agreed to (a) Page 26, line 31, leave out paragraph (c) and insert— “(c) the extent to which the design of the service, in particular its functionalities, affects the level of risk of harm that might be suffered by children, identifying and assessing those functionalities that present higher levels of risk, including a functionality that makes suggestions relating to users’ search requests (predictive search functionality);” Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (b) Page 26, line 33, at end insert— “(ca) the different ways in which the service is used, including functionalities or other features of the service that affect how much children use the service, and the impact of such use on the level of risk of harm that might be suffered by children;” Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (c) Page 26, line 35, leave out “(c)” and insert “(ca)” Lords Amendment 148 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to
chunks.display_text: or messaging content.” Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to (b) Page 9, line 38, after “used,” insert “including functionalities or other features of the service that affect how much children use the service (for example a feature that enables content to play automatically),” Lords Amendment 20 Secretary Michelle Donelan Agreed to To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Secretary Michelle Donelan To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Secretary Michelle Donelan Page 205, line 36, at end insert— Agreed to (a) 5 6 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS “Animal welfare 32A An offence under section 4(1) of the Animal Welfare Act 2006 (unnecessary suffering of an animal).” Lords Amendment 1 As Amendments to the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not called (a) Line 6, after “providers” insert “of significant size and capacity, and with a substantial involvement in the communication of media content,” Mr Marcus Fysh Not called (b) Line 22, at end insert— “(iv) systemic weakness and vulnerabilities relating to compliance with the duties are not introduced.” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 2 to 16, 18, 19, 21, and 23 to 39 agreed to. Lords Amendment 40 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Siobhan Baillie Mr David Davis Mr William Wragg Not selected Line 1, after ”effectively” insert— “(aa) enable a user to see which other users are verified and which are not,” Lords Amendment agreed to. (a) Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendments 41 to 80, 82 to 147, 149 to181, and 183 to 188 agreed to. Lords Amendment 189 Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh

chunks.text: Amendment agreed to. (a) Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendments 41 to 80, 82 to 147, 149 to181, and 183 to 188 agreed to. Lords Amendment 189 Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Withdrawn Page 81, line 1, leave out subsection (3) and insert— “(3) The power conferred by subsection (1) also includes power to require a person within any of paragraphs (a) to (d) of subsection (4) to take steps so that a person authorised by OFCOM is only able to view remotely— (a) (b) (3A) The power conferred by subsection (2A)— (a) (b) (c) (3B) information, using test data, demonstrating the operation of systems, processes or features, including functionalities and algorithms, used by the service; information, using test data, generated by a service by the performance of a test or demonstration of a kind required by a notice under subsection (1). must not be exercised in a way that leads to the creation of systemic weaknesses or vulnerabilities in systems, processes, functionalities or algorithms associated with any service; does not include any requirement to— (i) implement or build a new decryption capability, (ii) perform one or more actions that would render systemic methods of authentication or encryption less effective, or (iii) do any act or thing that would or may create a material risk that otherwise secure information would or may in the future be accessed, used, manipulated, disclosed or otherwise compromised by an unauthorised third party; and can only be exercised in relation to test data, and not in relation to data from consumers.
chunks.display_text: Amendment agreed to. (a) Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendments 41 to 80, 82 to 147, 149 to181, and 183 to 188 agreed to. Lords Amendment 189 Mr Marcus Fysh Not selected To move, That this House disagrees with the Lords in their Amendment. Mr Marcus Fysh To move the following Amendment to the Bill in lieu of the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Withdrawn Page 81, line 1, leave out subsection (3) and insert— “(3) The power conferred by subsection (1) also includes power to require a person within any of paragraphs (a) to (d) of subsection (4) to take steps so that a person authorised by OFCOM is only able to view remotely— (a) (b) (3A) The power conferred by subsection (2A)— (a) (b) (c) (3B) information, using test data, demonstrating the operation of systems, processes or features, including functionalities and algorithms, used by the service; information, using test data, generated by a service by the performance of a test or demonstration of a kind required by a notice under subsection (1). must not be exercised in a way that leads to the creation of systemic weaknesses or vulnerabilities in systems, processes, functionalities or algorithms associated with any service; does not include any requirement to— (i) implement or build a new decryption capability, (ii) perform one or more actions that would render systemic methods of authentication or encryption less effective, or (iii) do any act or thing that would or may create a material risk that otherwise secure information would or may in the future be accessed, used, manipulated, disclosed or otherwise compromised by an unauthorised third party; and can only be exercised in relation to test data, and not in relation to data from consumers.

chunks.text: thing that would or may create a material risk that otherwise secure information would or may in the future be accessed, used, manipulated, disclosed or otherwise compromised by an unauthorised third party; and can only be exercised in relation to test data, and not in relation to data from consumers. But the power conferred by subsection (1) must be exercised in a way that is necessary and proportionate to the use to which the information is to be put in the exercise of OFCOM’s functions.” (a) 7 8 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 190 to 216 agreed to. Lords Amendment 217 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Mr David Davis Caroline Lucas Not called (a) Not called (a) Line 7, after “sources” insert “, or (iii) circumvent end-to-end encryption on the service;” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 218 to 227 agreed to. Lords Amendment 228 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Line 10, at end insert— “(1C) A proactive technology requirement may not be used in a way that creation of systemic weaknesses or vulnerabilities in systems, processes, functionalities or algorithms associated with any service.” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 229 and 230 agreed to. Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendment 231 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Siobhan Baillie Mr David Davis Mr William Wragg Not called (a) Line 9, at end insert “and— (d) whether the inclusion of a requirement to enable a user to see which other users have verified their identity and which have not verified their identity would make the use of age assurance more effective for that purpose,” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 232 to 319 agreed to. Lords Amendment 320

But the power conferred by subsection (1) must be exercised in a way that is necessary and proportionate to the use to which the information is to be put in the exercise of OFCOM’s functions.” (a) 7 8 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendment agreed to. chunks.text: inclusion of a requirement to enable a user to see which other users have verified their identity and which have not verified their identity would make the use of age assurance more effective for that purpose,” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 232 to 319 agreed to. Lords Amendment 320 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not called (a) Line 1, after “bot” insert “except any transaction or data ordering bot used in a data exchange or multi-party computation system” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendment 321 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not called (a) Line 4, leave out “or images” insert “, images, programming code, transactions or data for exchange” 9 10 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 322 to 348, 350 to 390, and 392 to 424 agreed to. Glossary Agreed to: agreed without a vote. Agreed to on division: agreed following a vote. Not called: debated in a group of amendments, but not put to a decision. Not moved: not debated or put to a decision. Negatived: rejected without a vote. Not selected: not chosen for debate by the Speaker. Negatived on division: rejected following a vote. Withdrawn after debate: moved and debated but then withdrawn, so not put to a decision.
chunks.display_text: inclusion of a requirement to enable a user to see which other users have verified their identity and which have not verified their identity would make the use of age assurance more effective for that purpose,” Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 232 to 319 agreed to. Lords Amendment 321 As an Amendment to the Lords Amendment:— Mr Marcus Fysh Not called (a) Line 4, leave out “or images” insert “, images, programming code, transactions or data for exchange” 9 10 Tuesday 12 September 2023 LORDS AMENDMENTS Lords Amendment agreed to. Lords Amendments 322 to 348, 350 to 390, and 392 to 424 agreed to. Glossary Agreed to: agreed without a vote. Agreed to on division: agreed following a vote. Not called: debated in a group of amendments, but not put to a decision. Not moved: not debated or put to a decision. Negatived: rejected without a vote. Not selected: not chosen for debate by the Speaker. Negatived on division: rejected following a vote. Withdrawn after debate: moved and debated but then withdrawn, so not put to a decision.
